data.title: Deep Yellow Has Vastly Outperformed The Market, But Remains Attractive
data.description:
About four months ago, I recommended buying Deep Yellow (OTCQX:DYLLF) for its ideal positioning to benefit from an expected boom in nuclear energy. Since that article, the stock has vastly outperformed the broad market, as it has rallied 17% whereas the S&P 500 has declined 1%. However, the path of the stock price has been bumpy, as financial markets incurred a massive sell-off in the first months of the year. More importantly, after such a striking outperformance, it is only natural to wonder whether the stock is not attractive anymore. In this article, I will discuss the reasons behind the sensitivity of Deep Yellow to the global financial turmoil as well as why the stock remains attractive from a long-term perspective.

Business Overview

Uranium is the primary input of nuclear reactors and hence Deep Yellow, which is a producer of uranium, is highly sensitive to the prospects of nuclear power. The latter has fallen out of favor for approximately three decades due to the impact of a few catastrophic accidents on public sentiment. The accident in Chernobyl, Ukraine, in 1987 and the accident in Fukushima, Japan, in 2011, have caused cancer for thousands of people worldwide. Consequently, the generation of nuclear power worldwide has hardly grown over the last two decades.

However, the global energy crisis, which took place in 2022 in the aftermath of the sanctions of western countries on Russia for its invasion in Ukraine, is likely to prove a game changer with respect to nuclear power. Due to those sanctions, the global markets of oil and gas became extremely tight and thus the prices of oil and gas surged to 13-year highs in 2022. As a result, the costs of electricity and heating skyrocketed, leading numerous individuals around the globe to struggle to pay for their living costs.

That energy crisis led nearly all countries to find ways to diversify away from fossil fuels in order to reduce their vulnerability to a similar crisis in the future. Solar and wind projects have gained great popularity in the last three years, but they cannot generate power continuously, as their power is affected by the prevailing weather conditions. As a result, nuclear energy, which is considered a clean form of energy and is not affected by weather conditions, has gained popularity lately.

Source: Investor Presentation – May 2025

As global nuclear capacity has remained approximately flat over the last two decades and nuclear power is becoming critical as a part of the global energy transition away from fossil fuels, global nuclear capacity is likely to greatly increase in the future. As shown above, China expects to grow its nuclear capacity 7-fold by 2060 and the U.S. expects to triple its nuclear capacity by 2050. This trend is likely to provide a strong long-term tailwind for uranium producers, such as Deep Yellow.

However, before buying the stock, investors should be aware of the high sensitivity of the stock to the underlying economic conditions and the prospects of nuclear energy. The recent global financial turmoil, which triggered a massive sell-off in financial markets, revealed the sensitivity of the stock of Deep Yellow to the global economic outlook. While the S&P 500 plunged 20% from peak to bottom earlier this year, the stock of Deep Yellow slumped 46% from peak to bottom.

The reason behind the slump of the stock was the global trade war, which seemed to temporarily pause the boom in artificial intelligence. Data centers constitute a major growth driver for global energy consumption, as artificial intelligence consumes a great amount of energy. The International Energy Agency [IEA] expects the consumption of electricity by data centers all over the world to double between 2022 and 2026. A global trade war or a recession is likely to weigh on the growth rate of electricity consumption and hence it is likely to decelerate the growth of the use of nuclear power. This helps explain the plunge of Deep Yellow, which was triggered by the excessive tariffs declared by the U.S. government to most of its trading counterparts.

Fortunately, market sentiment has greatly improved lately, and financial markets have retrieved most of their losses, as the U.S. and China agreed to sharply reduce their tariffs during the weekend. The stock of Deep Yellow has greatly benefited from the softened stance of the U.S. government in the last four weeks, as it has enjoyed a 24% rally this year while the S&P 500 has shed 1% this year.

Some investors may claim that there is still high uncertainty related to the resolution of the global trade war. The U.S. government, which was remarkably aggressive until a few weeks ago, may become aggressive once again and may again impose excessive tariffs on other countries. As the current U.S. government has proved somewhat unpredictable in its moves, it would be hard to exclude a negative development in its negotiations with other countries.

Fortunately, the U.S. government recently realized that it is not free to threaten other countries with excessive tariffs. Instead, the U.S. government is a “hostage” of the global bond market. When the government announced its plan with excessive tariffs in early April, U.S. treasuries collapsed and their yield surged. It was a rare moment in financial history because U.S. bonds had almost always rallied in previous sell-offs of the stock market. Most of those who sold stocks used to buy U.S. bonds during panic periods of the stock market, but this time proved different. Those who sold stocks bought bonds of other countries, such as Germany, and thus U.S. bonds collapsed in tandem with stocks.

Higher bond yields mean that the U.S. would have to borrow funds at much higher interest rates. As a result, the annual interest expense of the U.S., which already stands at an all-time high, would increase even further and hence it would enhance the budget deficit of the country. In other words, all the efforts of the government to reduce the budget deficit would be wasted due to higher interest expense. As soon as the government realized this, it markedly softened its stance, and thus it gradually improved confidence in U.S. bonds.

To cut a long story short, the U.S. government is not likely to impose excessive tariffs, as it is a hostage of the global bond market. This is certainly positive for Deep Yellow, which is ideally positioned to benefit from healthy global economic growth, as the latter will provide ideal conditions for growth of global energy consumption and a boom in nuclear energy.

Deep Yellow has two major uranium projects, namely the Tumas project in Namibia and the Mulga Rock project in Western Australia, one of the largest uranium projects in Australia. The project in Namibia is expected to come online in 2027, it has a 30-year expected lifetime and has a net present value of $577 million at a uranium price of $82.5. The Mulga Rock project is expected to come online in 2029 and is expected to have a longer lifetime and a higher production than the Tumas project. I have discussed the two projects in more detail in the previous article.

Financial Position

Deep Yellow has a markedly strong balance sheet. To be sure, the company has a net cash position (as per Buffett’s formula: Net cash = cash + receivables – total liabilities) of $143 million. As a result, Deep Yellow does not pay any interest expense. Instead, it has posted net interest income of $5.8 million in the last 12 months.

The net cash position was $169 million six months ago. This means that the current cash burn rate is approximately $26 million per six months, or $52 million per year. As the Tumas project is expected to begin generating cash flows in 2027, the company seems to have sufficient cash until that year. Of course, as Deep Yellow will approach the date of commissioning of the project, it is likely to see its operating expenses increase. Therefore, the company may have to issue new shares and dilute its shareholders. Nevertheless, even in such an adverse scenario, the dilution is not likely to be excessive, given the ample cash right now.

It is also important to note that the stock has a market capitalization of $842 million, with a net cash position of $143 million. In other words, the market values the two projects of the company at $699 million. As mentioned above, the Tumas project has an estimated net present value of $577 million. The company has not provided an estimate for the Mulga Rock project, which will come online later than the Tumas project, but the Mulga Rock project is promising as well, as it is expected to have a longer lifetime and a higher production than the Tumas project.

Overall, the current stock valuation appears attractive when compared to the estimated value of the two projects. Of course, the future path of the stock will be determined to a great extent by future uranium prices, which are unpredictable. Nevertheless, the prospects for uranium prices are positive in the long run, given the expected boom in nuclear power.

Risks

Deep Yellow has promising prospects, but investors should be aware of two risks before positioning for a potential boom in nuclear power. First of all, as the two projects of the company are expected to begin generating cash flows in 2027 and 2029, there is an execution risk until these two deadlines. Nuclear projects can incur significant delays, which may cost material cost overruns. Southern (SO) is infamous for its nuclear plant that came online in 2023. That project materialized 8 years after the initial estimated date and cost more than twice as much as the initial cost estimate.

On the bright side, with only two years until the expected completion of the Tumas project, there is not as much margin for error in this project as there was in the project of Southern. Moreover, Deep Yellow has a healthy net cash position and hence it can probably handle some setbacks in its projects without any problem.

The other risk factor is the disastrous scenario of a fatal nuclear accident in the future in one of the 440 nuclear reactors that currently exist around the globe. In such a case, public sentiment over nuclear power is likely to become extremely negative once again, and thus the expected boom in nuclear power will be jeopardized. Fortunately, such a catastrophic scenario is highly unlikely.

Final Thoughts

The stock of Deep Yellow has vastly outperformed the S&P 500 this year, but it remains attractive thanks to the expected boom of nuclear energy in the long run. This form of energy remained out of favor for decades, but it is likely to prove a critical form of energy in the power supply of data centers thanks to its clean footprint. Investors should become positioned for the boom in nuclear power before this trend becomes too popular. Nevertheless, as Deep Yellow is still at least two years far from turning a profit, only patient investors, with a long-term perspective, should consider buying the stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Gold Slides 2.2% To One-Month Lows - $3150 Incoming?
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Gold prices have slipped around 2.2% today to trade at $2176 at the time of writing. It appears safe-haven appeal is finally waning thanks to a combination of factors.

US-China Deal

The talks over the weekend between the global powers were one of the first dominos which have kept Gold prices under pressure. Today, China's Commerce Ministry announced a pause on the unreliable entity list for 17 US firms and halted export controls on 28 entities for 90 days starting May 14.

Just another step toward easing the tensions.

President Trump's Gulf Trip

US President Donald Trump is on a four-day investment drive to the Gulf states, which so far has been impressive. Deals announced yesterday with Saudi Arabia include a $600 billion investment commitment in the US on top of arms sales to the Gulf state of around $142 billion.

This has been followed by today's announcements that Qatar will be investing in the US, and this started with a commitment to buying around 160 Boeing jets. The White House confirmed: “Qatar plans to invest in the US over the next five years.”

These announcements, while not trade deals exactly, will still inject a wee bit of optimism into financial markets which will no doubt weigh on haven demand.

Trade Deal Announcements Move Closer

On the trade deal front, there was a post by a Fox Reporter on X: “Trade-tariff deals with Japan and South Korea said to be “close,” according to Wall Street sources close to the White House. Timing unclear. India deals as well, though it has been throttled by the tensions with Pakistan.”

This would be another major step in the right direction on the trade deal front and will only add to Gold's woes should it come to fruition.

Russia-Ukraine Conflict

As for the Russia Ukraine conflict, there has been growing chatter, at least from a Ukrainian perspective, about an Istanbul meeting tomorrow.

For the third day in a row, the Kremlin hasn’t confirmed if Vladimir Putin will go to Istanbul on Thursday or who will represent Russia at the possible peace talks. Pressure is growing for Putin to meet with Volodymyr Zelenskyy.

Putin’s spokesperson, Dmitry Peskov, said they’ll announce the Russian delegation once Putin gives the go-ahead. “So far, no instructions have been given,” he said.

From the US, envoy Witkoff stated that he and Marco Rubio will go to Istanbul on Friday, but he is unsure if Putin will attend. If any positive developments materialize from the proposed talks, this could further strengthen the retreat from havens and weigh on Gold prices.

Market Sentiment

Looking at overall client sentiment at OANDA, 73% of traders are net-long on Gold.

You can see the OANDA traders’ positions sentiment for 20 most traded instruments for the last seven days and sort them by bullish or bearish bias expressed through their positions. If total short positions outweigh long positions, the clients appear to have a “bearish sentiment” against that instrument. Similarly, if long positions exceed short positions, customers are supporting a rising “bullish” trend.

I, however, hold a contrarian view of client sentiment. Given that 73% of traders are long on Gold there is a possibility the precious metal may continue to decline.

Technical Analysis - Gold (XAU/USD)

From a technical standpoint, Gold has broken through a key support area at 3200.

On the daily timeframe, Gold has broken below the period-14 RSI 50 level which hints at a change in momentum from bullish to bearish.

There is a key area around the 3170 handle, which was the swing high the day following President Trump's universal tariff announcement on April 2.

A break off this level could lead to a test of support at 3150 and 3125 respectively.

A move higher from here needs to find support above the 3195-3200 area before resistance may be found at around the 3233 and 3245 areas respectively.

Gold (XAU/USD) Daily Chart, May 14, 2025

Original Post

data.title: Tariff Tensions And Tactical Shifts In EM Debt
data.description:
While we retain a constructive medium- and long-term outlook for emerging markets (EM) debt, we have downgraded our near-term views for the asset class.

Tariff Turmoil

Prospects for global retaliation against U.S. President Donald Trump’s latest tariffs pose significant risks for the global economy. While real impacts are difficult to assess at this point, we believe uncertainty and instability should continue to cloud the outlook in the short term.

In the United States, higher tariffs on imported goods would likely lead to a temporary increase in inflation, pressuring consumer discretionary spending and economy activity. While we would not expect the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) to initially react to transitory inflationary pressures, the path toward monetary easing is uncertain.

Globally, higher tariffs could negatively impact trade. Countries running large trade surpluses with the United States would likely see the biggest impact on their economies. China, and to a lesser extent Europe, should be particularly affected. In these places, we expect significant fiscal and monetary policy reaction to offset the impacts of higher tariffs imposed by the U.S. administration. We would also expect strong retaliation, as tariffs could violate World Trade Organization (WTO) commitments, creating additional risks for global trade.

We believe EM countries are better positioned to withstand a global trade war than developed countries, as the universe should be less directly exposed given the significant growth in intra-EM trade observed over the past years.

China, for example, is likely to deploy fiscal and monetary stimulus, allow its currency to weaken, and continue redirecting exports away from the United States. EMs now account for nearly half of Chinese exports, and we expect trade between China, EMs, and other developed countries to increase.

A Challenging Market Environment

Despite the constructive fundamental backdrop in EM countries, we now believe U.S. policy uncertainty, a looming trade war, and unresolved geopolitical conflicts should result in a challenging market environment in the next few months.

As a result, we have adopted a more defensive stance in our portfolios. That said, we will look for opportunities to reposition as market volatility creates opportunities for long-term investors. Valuations have recently become more attractive after recent credit spread decompression. We prefer countries with stronger ability to withstand rising trade tariffs and continue to favor the ones with easier access to multilateral and bilateral funding.

Below, we break down some of our largest active hard-currency positions by beta bucket, which is how we allocate our risk budget.

High-Beta Bucket

In the high-beta bucket, we are overweight Ukraine, Sri Lanka, and Ecuador, and underweight Senegal, Angola, and Bolivia.

Ukraine (overweight): We’re overweight based on the potential for conflict negotiations, multilateral support, and a more optimistic growth outlook. We express this view through an overweight to Ukrainian warrants but recently trimmed our allocation to the B bonds on valuation grounds. We want to stay somewhat defensive in case optimism around conflict resolution proves misplaced or fades.

Sri Lanka (overweight): We are overweight macro-linked bonds, reflecting the positive trajectory of gross domestic product (GDP) growth, as shown in the third and fourth quarter 2024 real GDP prints. We also believe the International Monetary Fund’s (IMF’s) successful third review of Sri Lanka’s Extended Fund Facility (EFF) program should further boost investor confidence in the country’s economic outlook.

Ecuador (overweight): Valuations are appealing. Markets were pricing in a first-round win for incumbent Daniel Noboa, but first-round results showed almost a dead tie between Noboa and Luisa Gonzalez. Since no candidate secured a majority in the first round, there will be a run-off, and markets seem to be pricing in a much higher probability of Luisa Gonzalez winning, which we think offers asymmetric upside.

Senegal (underweight): Senegal has seen upward revisions to public debt and fiscal deficit figures over the past five years. Fiscal performance sharply deteriorated in 2024, and the 2025 budget’s planned consolidation faces significant implementation risks. While bond valuations have improved following the underperformance, we see limited scope for outperformance without more credible policy measures to restore fiscal credibility. We continue to monitor Senegal’s discussions with the IMF for signs of progress toward a funded program.

Angola (underweight): We are underweight in spread-duration terms but overweight in cash, primarily through a short-dated amortizing bond. While we remain cautious on downside risks to oil prices, we believe Angola is both willing and able to meet its external obligations in the near term.

Bolivia (underweight): We have credit concerns, viewing Bolivia’s policy mix—large fiscal deficits and a pegged exchange rate—as unsustainable amid low and falling reserves and a current account deficit. Persistent foreign currency shortages in 2024 highlight these risks. Given the weak fundamentals and unattractive valuations, we remain on the sidelines.

Medium-Beta Bucket

In the medium-beta bucket, we are overweight Colombia, Guatemala, and Mexico, and underweight Bahrain, Dominican Republic, and Costa Rica.

Colombia (overweight): Valuations are appealing. Colombia had underperformed on concerns over its widening fiscal deficit, but we now see the bonds as inexpensive relative to peers. President Gustavo Petro’s low approval ratings also point to a likely regime change in the 2026 elections, which could further support sentiment.

Guatemala (overweight): Valuations are appealing, fundamentals are strong, and Guatemala appears to be on a positive rating trajectory, largely due to low debt, stable and low deficits, and a strong growth trajectory.

Mexico (overweight): We are overweight Pemex, given strong sovereign support and attractive valuations relative to the sovereign. However, we are underweight the Mexican sovereign on valuation grounds. We do hold positions in select financials and airlines.

Bahrain (underweight): We are concerned about volatility in regional geopolitics, the country’s weak fiscal-reform efforts, lower oil prices, and tight valuations.

Dominican Republic (underweight): Valuations are not compelling despite the country appearing to be on a positive fundamental trajectory.

Costa Rica (underweight): Fundamentals are strong, but we believe valuations are unattractive because spreads have compressed materially.

Low-Beta Bucket

In the low-beta bucket, we are overweight Paraguay, Trinidad and Tobago, and the United Arab Emirates, and underweight China, Indonesia, and Uruguay.

Paraguay (overweight): Valuations are attractive relative to low-beta peers and fundamentals are solid. The country’s debt-to-GDP ratio remains low, and the country has a strong history of low fiscal deficits.

Trinidad and Tobago (overweight): We recently moved to an overweight position due to the country’s more appealing valuations relative to its low-beta peers.

United Arab Emirates (overweight): Our overweight position is driven predominantly by a combination of corporates and the smaller emirates, where we believe valuations are attractive relative to Abu Dhabi.

China (underweight): Valuations are tight and we see unpredictable regulatory risks to Chinese state-owned entities. Based on bottom-up analysis, we hold selective corporate bonds at attractive valuations and positive credit trajectories.

Indonesia (underweight): Valuations are unappealing, and the fundamental outlook has become less clear following the February 2024 presidential elections. With the new administration in place as of October, there’s a risk of fiscal slippage as priorities shift from stability to growth. The country is also expected to be a sizable external debt issuer in 2025. That said, we hold a diversified set of corporate positions, supported by resilient fundamentals across financials, renewable utilities, and oil and gas.

Uruguay (underweight): We remain underweight on valuations, as Uruguay trades among the tightest of Latin America’s low-beta countries.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Lantheus Holdings: Poor Results Caused By Temporary Problems - A Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Lantheus (NASDAQ:LNTH) shares fell 25% after its Q1 2025 report. While it is never good to disappoint expectations and reduce EPS guidance for 2025, I think it is justified by M&A costs, divestitures of a low-growth segment and low margins; therefore it will likely be a temporary headwind and this drop could be an entry opportunity. If management forecasts are met, double-digit growth should return by 2026.

EPS Guidance Reduction

In the first week of May, Lantheus released its Q1 2025 report. The stock subsequently fell nearly 25%, and I think there were some reasons for this drop. First, the results were worse than analysts expected, with reported sales of $373 million (1.7% less than expected) and representing year-over-year growth of only 0.8%. The reported adj EPS of $1.53 million (7.5% less than expected) didn't fare much better either, falling nearly 10% year-over-year.

Estimate Actual Difference
Revenue $379M $373M -1.7%
EBIT $153M $144M -5.8%
EPS $1.65 $1.53 -7.5%

This modest growth was significantly impacted by flat sales of PYLARIFY, a radiotracer used in the evaluation of prostate cancer, which accounted for nearly 70% of sales in Q1 2025. While volume increased slightly, indicating demand for these products, the company had to reduce prices because its previous pass-through status under Medicare allowed it to be paid the full price for the drug and procedure. This status expired, and the company switched to a mean unit cost model, where centers now receive an average reimbursement, often lower than the actual acquisition cost.

As a result of this change in Medicare coverage in Lantheus's core segment, profitability suffered, starting with gross margins. But I believe the real cause of the stock's decline was a change in guidance for FY 2025. While sales remained within the expected range through the end of fiscal year 2024 ($1.545B – $1.610B), there was a reduction in EPS of approximately 6%.

Strategic Transformation

To understand the changes in guidance and the future of Lantheus, we need to address two key points: M&A and divestitures.

Earlier this month, Lantheus announced the sale of a portion of its SPECT business to Illuminated Holdings. This SPECT (Single Photon Emission Computed Tomography) segment is an older form of imaging than the PET used in the rest of Lantheus' products, so the company hadn't focused on these products within its portfolio for some time. Mr. Marshall, Lantheus' CFO, commented that this business segment represents between $115M and $120M of revenue, but the margins are lower, and during Q1 2025, its sales decreased by almost double digits, so it seems like a strategically good sale.

If the idea was only to sell the SPECT segment to improve profitability, it would seem reasonable, but the company doesn't expect that alone and is working on M&A to continue strengthening its PET solutions. Specifically, with the acquisitions of Life Molecular Imaging (referred to as LMI) for an upfront payment of $350M and additional payments of up to $400M and Evergreen Theragnostics for $250M and an additional $752M in potential milestone payments that management expects will come from share dilution.

Both were announced in January 2025 and are on a mission to expand Lantheus' presence into other, more lucrative market segments such as Alzheimer's and cancer diagnostics. These products have the potential to generate synergies with other Lantheus products to not only diagnose but also treat certain problems. For example, the drug PNT2003, which is about to be launched, is a radioligand therapy that directly targets tumors. Thus, a hospital could use OCTEVY (from Evergreen) or Lantheus' own PYLARIFY to diagnose prostate cancer or neuroendocrine tumors and then also use the aforementioned PNT2003 to treat them. This would allow for both diagnosis and treatment in a single patient.

Valuation

This year, guidance is for a modest 2% growth, and the divestment of the SPECT segment is not yet reflected. By 2026, management has commented that they expect to return to double-digit growth thanks to acquisitions, but also due to the upcoming pipeline. Considering the synergies that could be achieved with these acquisitions, I think this target is realistic, and if margins improve now that the SPECT segment has been divested, then net margins of 25% (similar to those in 2023) could be achieved again.

In the near term, the company would be trading at 12 times its 2025 adjusted earnings guidance and nearly 20 times unadjusted EPS (I'm not so keen on the addition of stock-based compensation). In any case, with long-term growth returning and margin improvements, I see Lantheus generating $684M in net income or just over $9 in EPS considering the share dilution from the Evergreen acquisition (75M shares outstanding by 2029). At a P/E of 20 times, that would represent a share price of over $180 or doubling from the current price.

Risks

In my previous coverage, I discussed risks related to patents and generic competition. Today, another risk has been added to the list: aggressive regulation.

On May 12, 2025, following a series of social media posts, President Donald Trump announced an executive order to implement a "most-favored-nation" pricing policy for prescription drugs. This policy seeks to align drug prices in the United States with the lowest among developed nations, with the goal of significantly reducing costs for American patients.

The MFN policy could lower drug prices, which could affect Lantheus's margins, especially if its products are subject to this policy. While there is still much uncertainty about the specific application of the MFN policy to drugs and healthcare programs (such as Medicare and Medicaid), this is already complicating matters for the company at a time when the market had already taken its toll due to its apparently poor recent results.

The good news is that the policy gives drug manufacturers a six-month negotiation period before mandatory pricing is implemented. We've already seen Trump's negotiation method (145% tariffs on China, later lowered to 30%), so we'll have to wait for the true impact of this new policy on Lantheus's profits. One more risk to add to the list.

The Bottom Line

I think these seemingly poor results are justified by the interesting strategic move management is making. The Alzheimer's and oncology areas seem like a good segment to explore in the future, and the potential synergies make the investment and the temporary weak results worthwhile. Volatility is not unusual at Lantheus and the shares had already fallen sharply during Q3 2024 and ended up rising 25% in the four months following my article (although the gains have been erased by this report).

For this reason, I think the company is providing another entry point at a reasonable price.

data.title: iTeos Therapeutics: End To Belrestotug Program Leads To A Hold Rating
data.description:
iTeos Therapeutics Overview

The last time I wrote about iTeos Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:ITOS) was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "iTeos: Readouts From TIGIT and Adenosine Pathway Inhibitors Makes This A Must-Watch." At that time, I noted the potential of the company advancing its anti-TIGIT drug belrestotug together with GSK plc's (GSK) PD-1/PD-L1 JEMPERLI (dostarlimab) for the targeting of previously untreated (1st-lline), unresectable, locally advanced, or metastatic PD-L1 high NSCLC patients. The company was set to release data from this phase 2 GALAXIES Lung-201 study in order to see if its anti-TIGIT molecule combined with PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor JEMPERLI could achieve a marked improvement for the treatment of these patients with advanced NSCLC.

Unfortunately, investors got some bad news as it relates to the development of this specific program. It was noted that this mid-stage study did not achieve a clinically meaningful improvement in terms of progression-free survival in this patient population. With that being said, the company has moved to increase shareholder value by enacting a strategic review. Based on recent events, I am now shifting the rating of this stock to a “Hold” rating from a "Buy" rating. If this company only had belrestotug left, then I would be inclined to move this to a "Sell" rating. However, it still has two other drugs left in its pipeline, which are EOS-984 and EOS-215.

Both of these drugs are in phase 1 trials at the moment, but if successful, could still yield some value for shareholders. Thus, while not guaranteed, it is possible that it might be able to at least at the very least garner some type of merger agreement with another pharmaceutical company that might be interested in either of these two oncology assets. Primarily, EOS-984, which targets ENT1, may have an effect on inhibiting the adenosine pathway. Why this might yield value while a strategic review has been enacted is because data from it is slated for the 2nd half of 2025.

End Of Belrestotug Development Means Move To Strategic Review

As I stated above, iTeos Therapeutics and its partner GlaxoSmithKline have agreed to terminate further development of belrestotug + dostarlimab doublet therapy for the treatment of patients with previously untreated, unresectable, locally advanced, or metastatic PD-L1 high non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) patients. The reason for such a termination was based on data evaluated from the ongoing phase 2 GALAXIES Lung-201 study. This specific mid-stage study had an estimated enrollment of 340 patients. Such patients were to be randomized to receive either of the following doses:

  • Belrestotug + JEMPERLI (dostarlimab)
  • JEMPERLI.

One thing to note is that these 1st-line metastatic NSCLC patients had to have high levels of PD-L1. In essence, they had to have a tumor proportion score (TPS) of >= 50%. The primary endpoint of this study was set to be as Objective Response Rate (ORR). While the secondary endpoint was established as Progression-Free Survival (PFS). I would say that, based on the data released, the drug itself did achieve one positive result. What positive would that be? Well, in terms of the primary endpoint of ORR, there was a clinically meaningful improvement shown. That was the good news; however, the bad news was that a far more important clinical endpoint of PFS did not achieve a clinically meaningful improvement over that of dostarlimab alone as a monotherapy.

The truth is that one study alone would not be enough, for both iTeos and its partner GlaxoSmithKline chose to terminate further development of belrestotug. With that being said, there was a glimpse shown in another study that both companies were working on. This would be a phase 2 study, known as GALAXIES H&N-202. This specific study was evaluating belrestotug combinations versus monotherapy dostarlimab to target PD-L1 positive head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC) patients. In this situation, not even the ORR threshold was being reached with the belrestotug combination compared to that of monotherapy dostarlimab. With that being said, all cohorts are being ended, along with the phase 3 GALAXIES Lung-301 trial as well. These events here are why I have shifted the rating from a Buy to a Hold rating. Again, it goes back to the notion that the company didn't only have belrestotug in its pipeline.

If this were the only drug left in its pipeline, then I would put a Sell rating. However, with two ongoing phase 1 studies in the pipeline, it is possible there is still some redeeming value left. Also, the company is not just going to press on with these two candidates. Instead, it believes that it has a better chance to create shareholder value by enacting a full range of strategic alternatives.

It is not clear what the final outcome of this could end up being, but in these situations, a lot of biotech companies look to establish some type of merger agreement with another company. This might be possible thanks to the fact that iTeos has a significant amount of cash on hand. As a matter of fact, it had a cash balance and investment balance of $624.3 million as of March 31st of 2025. With the belrestotug program being wounded down to preserve cash, I believe that in the end it could at least enact some type of merger agreement to generate shareholder value.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, iTeos Therapeutics had cash balance and investment balance of $624.3 million as of March 31, 2025. The truth is that this company was already set up for an extended period of time to operate its business. Why do I state that? It is because it is guided in its 10-Q SEC Filing that it would have enough cash on hand for a cash runway to fund its operations through 2027. It truly had enough cash to get several phase 3 studies going if the belrestotug + dostarlimab programs panned out. It was burning approximately $40 million in cash per quarter.

With the failure of the belrestotug program, it now has to enact a strategic alternative to generate shareholder value. As I have laid out here, though, it has plenty of cash on hand to enact some type of transaction to maximize shareholder value. Especially, in light of the fact that it stopped any further funding of its belrestotug clinical development program.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in iTeos Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing phase 1 study using EOS-984 for the treatment of patients with advanced malignancies. The company was expected to release data from this early-stage trial using this drug to target these patients in the 2nd half of 2025. The hope is that by targeting the ENT1 molecule transporter, it would then result in an increase of T-cell proliferation (expansion) in the tumor microenvironment (TME) and beyond. The risk here is that there is no assurance that any such data revealed from this study targeting these advanced solid tumor patients with EOS-984 will end up being positive. Nor that the mechanism of action (MOA) of targeting the ENT1 transporter molecule result in the proliferation of T-cells to increase efficacy in targeting such patients.

The second risk to consider would then be in terms of the second drug candidate it is developing in a phase 1 study, which is an anti-TREM2 agonist, EOS-215. The goal of this drug is to target TREM2 and possibly overcome PD-1 resistance in solid tumor patients. The risk here is that upon any release of data from this early-stage study, there is no guarantee that it will end up being positive. Nor that the company ultimately be able to advance to a phase 2 clinical study for further evaluation.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the strategic alternatives enacted by iTeos. The goal is to maximize shareholder value by enacting a transaction such as a merger, selling off assets, or another notable item. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the initiation of a strategic review by TD Cowen result in a strategic transaction being done. If the company fails to merge or enact some type of transaction with another company, then it will be forced to rely on its phase 1 clinical studies ongoing of EOS-215 and EOS-984 to build shareholder value. There is no assurance that either of these candidates is going to attain positive data, for starters. Nor, that either one will advance to the next stages of clinical testing to continue to build shareholder value.

Conclusion

iTeos Therapeutics did not expect its development of belrestotug + dostarlimab in targeting patients with PD-L1-positive and PD-L1-high solid tumors to fail. With that being said, it makes sense for this biotech to end further progress for the development of anti-TIGIT belrestotug going forward. My logical reasoning for maintaining a Hold rating on this company's stock is because of the potential to enact a strategic alternative like a merger or something closely similar to it. Especially when you consider that it is quite flush with loads of cash.

As I have stated above in the “Financials” subsection of this article, it had approximately $624.3 million as of March 31, 2025. This is estimated to be enough to allow it to fund its operations through 2027. This is plenty of time to allow it to generate shareholder value through some type of strategic transaction. Despite the failure of the combination of belrestotug + dostarlimab for the treatment of patients with advanced solid tumors, plus GSK ending its development pact with this small-cap biotech, I believe that iTeos deserves a Hold rating on maximizing shareholder value through some type of strategic alternative.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

For Investor A shares: Total 0.97%; Net, Including Investment Related Expenses 0.95%. Institutional and Investor A shares have contractual waivers with an end date of 06/30/2026 terminable upon 90 days' notice. For certain share classes, BlackRock may voluntarily agree to waive certain fees and expenses in which the adviser may discontinue at any time without notice. Expenses stated as of the fund’s most recent prospectus. Data represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Investment returns and principal values may fluctuate so that an investor s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. All returns assume reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current performance may be lower or higher than that shown. Refer to blackrock.com for most recent month-end performance. Investment returns reflect total fund operating expenses, net of all fees, waivers and/or expense reimbursements. Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index. Share classes have different sales charges, fees and other features. Returns with sales charge reflect deduction of current maximum initial sales charge of 2.5% for Investor A shares. Institutional shares have no front- or back-end load. Institutional shares have limited availability and may be purchased at various minimums. See prospectus for details. Net Expenses Excluding Investment Related Expenses for Institutional shares: 0.68%; for Investor A shares: 0.92%.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of 0.24% (Institutional shares) and 0.07% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • The fund’s overweight exposure to higher-quality BB rated securities and strong selection among those rated CCC contributed to returns, while selection among B rated securities detracted. The chemicals, consumer cyclical service, and cable & satellite sectors benefited performance, while the technology and aerospace & defense sectors hampered returns.
  • The fund had an up-in-quality position, with an overweight exposure to BB rated securities and underweight positions in those rated B3 and CCC. We selectively added risk during the quarter. From a sector-positioning perspective, the fund had underweight allocations to retail, consumer cyclicals, and technology, and overweight positions in diversified manufacturing and aerospace & defense. Exchange-traded funds and swaps were utilized for efficiency.

Contributors

Detractors

The fund’s overweight position in higher-quality BB rated securities was additive, as this segment of the market generated positive performance. Additionally, strong selection among CCC rated securities significantly contributed to performance. From a sector standpoint, selection within chemicals, consumer cyclical services, and cable & satellite was a notable driver of the fund’s return.

From an asset class perspective, an out-of- benchmark allocation to loan index products had the most significant negative impact on performance. Additionally, selection within the term loan category weighed on returns, as did investment decisions among B rated securities. Similarly, selection in the technology and aerospace & defense sectors negatively impacted performance.

Further insight

Tariff-induced market volatility has led to a broad recalibration across risk assets. Bank loans have held up well, with loan spreads widening by about 70 basis points (BPs) to the mid-to-high 400bps level between February and mid-April. We expect elevated volatility to persist amid a challenging economic growth, inflation, and policy mix. Assuming no further news, we anticipate that the economy will slow, with tariffs impacting households and corporates. Loan spreads widening to the low 500bps range seem reasonable. Importantly, loan total returns remain resilient due to attractive “carry” and sound fundamentals, with an average coupon of about 7.75% helping to provide a buffer against price declines.

★★★★ Morningstar OverallTM

Holdings shown should not be deemed as a recommendation to buy or sell securities. Corporate loan values fluctuate in price so your investment can go down depending on market conditions. Corporate loans may be illiquid, affecting the fund’s ability to realize net asset value in the event of a liquidation of assets. The fund may invest in non-U.S. borrowers, which involves risks including fluctuation in foreign exchange rates, political and economic developments. Corporate loans in the fund’s portfolio typically are not rated or rated non-investment-grade (below Baa by Moody’s or BBB by Standard & Poor’s). These corporate loans generally involve greater risks to principal and income. The fund may use derivatives to hedge its investments or to seek to enhance returns. Derivatives entail risks relating to liquidity, leverage and credit that may reduce returns and increase volatility.

Information and opinions are derived from proprietary and nonproprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy.

BlackRock provides compensation in connection with obtaining or using third-party ratings and rankings.

The performance of the Institutional share class would be substantially similar to Investor A shares because the Institutional share class and Investor A shares invest in the same portfolio of securities and performance would only differ to the extent that the Institutional share class and Investor A shares have different expenses. See the fund prospectus for more details. 2 The S&P/LSTA Leveraged Loan Index is a market-weighted index that tracks the performance of institutional leveraged loans by monitoring spreads and interest payments to gauge the overall health of the institutional loan markets.†† The Morningstar RatingTM for funds, or "star rating", is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product s monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its three-, five-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% three-year rating for 36-59 months of total returns, 60% five-year rating/40% three-year rating for 60-119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% five-year rating/20% three-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent three-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods. data.title: The Leading Economy: The First Step In Tracking The Business Cycle
data.description:
In our last article, we explored the broader framework that underpins our process for tracking the Business Cycle — the EPB Four Economy Framework.

(Click here to read the previous post)

In that overview, we established that while the Aggregate Economy represents the overall economic picture and “defines” the Business Cycle, different sectors move at different times.

Some sectors always lead the broader economy, and some sectors always lag.

The good news is that the sequence is the same in every Business Cycle. Understanding this sequence is, therefore, crucial in making sense of where the economy stands today and where we might be headed next.

It’s a repeatable, step-by-step process. If you know you are in the second step of the process, you can pay particular attention to step three. This allows for a crystal clear focus and massively helps to reduce noise, confusion, and conflicting opinions.

The last post was an overview of the entire process.

In this post, we’ll focus specifically on the first step, tracking the Leading Economy.

The Leading Economy: The First Glimpse Into The Future

In the EPB Four Economy Framework, the most critical part of the last three buckets is the labor market. The labor market is the ultimate driver of the economy.

The Leading Economy is the origin of the Business Cycle sequence.

The Leading Economy doesn't measure growth or employment directly, but rather the conditions that determine the future path of growth and employment.

These indicators offer an early warning system, providing valuable insight into whether economic momentum is likely to strengthen or weaken in the coming months.

The Leading Economy measures the availability of money & credit, as well as forward-looking business commitments and sales of key Cyclical Economy items.

Let’s look at a few specific Leading Economy items, starting with the most leading, real money supply.

At EPB Research, in our weekly publications, we track several measures of money supply, as there are different monetary aggregates that provide different information.

Below, we are looking at the inflation-adjusted Austrian measure of money supply called TMS-2. This is a broad monetary aggregate that removes some items that cannot be instantly converted to cash, like bank CDs and money market funds. These instruments must be converted into a checking account before being spent.

The Federal Reserve started to raise interest rates in the summer of 2004. The increase in interest rates slowed the rate of real money creation—which is the goal.

Real money supply peaked in January 2005 and trended flat-to-down through 2008. The economy requires increasing real money supply to grow the unit volume of production and consumption, so this flattening and decline in real money supply created tight conditions.

Shortly after the peak in real money supply came a peak in another Leading Economy indicator—new home sales.

New home sales peaked in the summer of 2005 and headed down sharply through 2008.

Similarly, applications for new building permits peaked in September 2005 and headed down forcefully throughout 2006 and 2007.

While housing was at the center of the 2008 Business Cycle, new heavy truck sales, a key indicator of future motor vehicle manufacturing, peaked in early 2006 and declined through 2007 and 2008.

The yield curve also inverted in early 2006 and stayed inverted throughout 2007.

Putting it all together, by early 2006, two years before the onset of the Aggregate Economy recession, there was a peak in real money supply, an inversion of the yield curve, a peak in new home sales, building permits, and heavy truck sales.

While this is not an exhaustive list of Leading Economy indicators, it clearly demonstrates the benefits of the Four Economy Framework and why grouping indicators that are all supposed to move at the same time is powerful.

A “cluster” of peaks and subsequent declines in a collective group of indicators that are all leading in nature highlights that monetary policy is tight and declines in employment are likely to come.

This is how to measure whether monetary policy is “tight” or “easy,” not the stock market as it is commonly used today.

By the middle of 2006, the tightness of monetary policy was abundantly clear, yet the stock market soared for more than another year, highlighting why stocks are not a leading indicator in the modern era.

The key point is that these Leading Economy indicators do not measure actual production or employment. However, they always move ahead of the key economic turning points, making them powerful tools for anticipating changes before they show up in broader economic data.

Why the Leading Economy Matters

The Leading Economy or Leading Indicators are not “magic” or voodoo. Many people mistake that Leading Indicators can sometimes work and sometimes fail.

The nature of Leading Indicators is far more mechanical.

While it’s true that the lag time from when Leading Indicators impact actual employment can vary, making changes seem like a random event, it’s genuinely much more scientific.

Lead and lag times are, as the saying goes, sometimes long and variable, but the sequence is mechanical and highly repeatable.

When monetary conditions tighten and are matched with a decline in forward-looking indicators like building permits and the volume of new home sales, it’s a strong signal that economic momentum is set to slow.

Conversely, when money and credit expand and forward indicators like new orders rise, future production and employment tend to follow.

This phase of the cycle is often misunderstood because it does not yet reflect the conditions that people experience in their day-to-day lives.

If the Leading Economy is weakening, the labor market may still appear strong, and the Aggregate Economy may still be growing.

However, waiting until economic weakness becomes visible in the Aggregate Economy often means reacting too late. By then, the major shifts in investment conditions had already taken place.

The First Phase Transition: Leading to Cyclical

The Leading Economy sets the stage for what comes next. If Leading Economy indicators start to roll over, the Cyclical Economy—the next phase of our framework, will likely weaken in the future.

This sequence is why tracking the Business Cycle in a structured and repeatable manner is so critical.

By focusing on the Leading Economy first, we can position ourselves ahead of economic shifts, ensuring that we are prepared for changes in business conditions and investment opportunities before they become obvious to the consensus.

data.title: IDEXX Laboratories: Growth Remains Despite Weak Clinical Visits
data.description:
Since I upgraded IDEXX Laboratories, Inc. (NASDAQ:IDXX) to a Buy rating in March 2025, the stock price has increased by more than 22%. The company delivered a strong quarterly result despite a decline in U.S. clinical visits. I reiterate a Buy rating with a fair value of $523 per share.

IDEXX released its Q1 FY25 results on May 1st, delivering 4.7% organic revenue growth and 7% adj. EPS growth, as detailed in the table below. As discussed in my previous article, the number of U.S. clinical visits has been under pressure in recent quarters due to the weak economy. It is evident that consumers are reducing some discretionary spending. Despite the pressure from clinical visits, IDEXX continued to deliver strong organic growth with 90bps adj. operating margin expansions.

I think there are several catalysts for IDEXX’s stock price in the near future. Firstly, IDEXX is a US-centric business, with more than 70% of revenues coming from the North American market. The potential corporate tax cut could benefit IDEXX’s profit growth, as the company is currently paying more than 21% non-GAAP tax rate currently.

Secondly, I anticipate the U.S. consumer spending will gradually normalize in the near future, as the inflation starts to cool down and the low oil prices benefit U.S. consumers. A normalized consumption could increase the overall U.S. clinical visits for pets, which could benefit IDEXX’s topline growth. During the call, the management anticipates a 2% decline in U.S. clinical visits in FY25, a similar level to FY24. It’s worth noting that the management’s estimate is based on the current economic condition, and the growth rate will be too conservative, in my view, if the U.S. consumption gradually normalizes in the second half of FY25.

Lastly, the company’s Diagnostic business has strong pricing power over its customers, achieving an average of 4% pricing increase globally during the quarter. The strong pricing power could enable IDEXX to offset any potential cost pressures.

During the earnings call, the management noted that the tariff impact is quite limited for IDEXX, as the company is a US-centric operation, with diversified sourcing partners.

For FY25, the company is guiding for 6%-9% organic revenue growth and 8%-12% adj. EPS growth, as detailed in the table below:

Due to the weak U.S. clinic visits in FY25, I anticipate IDEXX’s total revenue will grow by 8.2% in FY25, incorporating 8% organic revenue growth, 1.2% growth from acquisitions and -1% impact from FX. From FY26 onwards, I forecast the company’s organic revenue growth will normalize to 10%, aligned with their historical average level. In addition, I assume the company will allocate 3% of revenue to acquisitions, contributing 120bps to the topline growth.

I model 30bps margin expansion, driven by 10bps from the pricing increase and 20bps from a reduction in SG&A. The total operating expenses are calculated to grow by 10.7% annually, leading to a 32% operating margin by FY34.

DCF and FCFE:

The cost of equity is calculated to be 9.9%, assuming: risk-free 4.2%; beta 0.95; equity risk premium 6%. I set the terminal growth to 5%, assuming 2% pricing growth and 3% volume growth. The tax rate is assumed to be 21.5% in the model. Discounting all the future FCFE, the fair value is calculated to be $523 per share, as per my estimates.

Key Risks

I think the biggest risk for IDEXX lies in the overall consumer sentiment. While I am optimistic about the normalization of consumer spending, people could continue to cut their discretionary spending on their pets. As discussed in my previous article, IDEXX has been promoting its cancer diagnostic business with the IDEXX Cancer Dx solution. The cancer testing is quite discretionary in nature, and consumers might not opt for these types of tests if they have a tight cash flow. In that case, IDEXX’s organic revenue growth might get impacted.

Conclusion

I think a normalized consumer spending environment will benefit IDEXX’s business growth in the near future. I reiterate a Buy rating on IDEXX with a fair value of $523 per share.
